item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be found under any source as cc-compliant. Thus, it is assumed that the image is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not really feeling it, but I can see how people would like it.

Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is only one thing that bothers me.

The "unidentified" nature of the tree.

It breaks my suspension of disbelief that the Foundation would not at least try to run genetic identification of the clippings they take from the potted bonsai tree.

And if they have, and the results were inconclusive (a jumble of DNA from multiple tree species, perhaps?) than it seems far more likely to me that would be a fine footnote worth adding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Human shaper tree, Wanderer's library… So upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bonsai- from wikipedia…

The practice of bonsai is sometimes confused with dwarfing, but dwarfing generally refers to research, discovery, or creation of plant cultivars that are permanent, genetic miniatures of existing species. Bonsai does not require genetically dwarfed trees, but rather depends on growing small trees from regular stock and seeds.

Now, look at what the SCP is doing to people. "Makes retarded babies" is only a surface understanding of what's going on here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So the tree reimpregnates women over their existing pregnancies with retarded babies? Also, I don't see how the babies resemble bonsais, so you should put that in the description or cut the line about them looking like their father out of the interview. I suggest the former, because a bonsai tree that just makes retarded babies doesn't make sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i'm with the others in that the "bonsai'ed humans" hook needs to be better described, make it easier for people to come to that conclusion without being horrible people like scantron and voct <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi, sorry for a late reply. No, I did not hear the kitten hoax, but it was vaguely inspired by some cartoon I saw many years ago.

I did not want to describe the children in morbid details and left it to imagination. I tried to imply that there was something esthetic about them (It is said in the interview that the mother finds them beautiful ''like their father''). But most people would not see them that way, so it's not stated clearly in the main text.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bonsai humans.

The very concept alone is worth an upvote.

Question…was this inspired by the infamous "bonsai kittens" hoax?

My only criticism is that the bonsai humans aren't described as an…aesthetic, for lack for a better word. Bonsais are cultivated for their visual balance, proportions, and often do what is called "lignification" in order to make them look like very old trees. These children are simply severely disabled little people-and that's fairly mundane. They should be different, special in some way that creates a sense of the uncanny, a sense of beauty mixed with horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm going to guess that you have a cat.

item.title: Re: On the edge of upvoting...
item.content:encoded: I just feel that the interview needs to be rewritten. The article itself is alright, the interview really feels like it doesn't fit, and doesn't have nearly the same quality of writing as the rest of the article did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For now, no vote. That will turn into an upvote if you have something in yiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiuuuuuuuyhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhjjjjjjjjhyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyynnnnnnnnnnnbn the main body of the text explaining how it makes people into bonsai- 'malformities' doesn't give it any detail. It took me four rereads of the interview to figure out what was happening and then I came here to confirm that I got the twist.
Great concept, decent execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly the body was boring but the interview made me downvote. This isn't scary, it isn't interesting, and it's just very bland. The interview lasted for a few seconds, after which she just randomly ceased providing information? It doesn't make sense and it feels like the interviewer just decided "Screw it, I wasn't curious anyway."

item.title: Re: On the edge of upvoting...
item.content:encoded:
Sorry for the late reply, but I haven't been on the site for a while.

These are very good considerations. Since this was my first successful SCP, at the time I wanted to play it safe and keep it as short-and-sweet as possible. I just wanted to convey the idea of a bonsai tree that make people-bonsai. The interview log was meant more to make sure people understood the idea. My personal head canon is that this is the only human instance as far the Foundation knows and they don't have enough tests on human subjects yet to make conclusions about how it affects humans.

Also, thinking back, maybe I should remove the mental retardation. I put this because it is usually part of severe development defects like the ones described. But in that case, it does not need to be part of the effects. I think I put it there just to simplify things and not have to put interview logs of those children directly.

I think the story of the mother or the child could make an interesting Tale and maybe I will try it sometime. I wrote a tale on the Wanderer's Library vaguely concerning the origin of that tree, but it does not really give any important details about it's effects.

item.title: Re: On the edge of upvoting...
item.content:encoded: Remember, you should edit your previous posts instead of double-posting, using the "edit" function under the "options" tab to the lower right of every comment. It's in the rules. :)

item.title: Re: On the edge of upvoting...
item.content:encoded: [edited in to the post above]

item.title: On the edge of upvoting...
item.content:encoded:
I feel like I'm on the verge of upvoting this article, but there is something that feels… lacking about it, outside of the interview.

There's not enough of a… sinister, otherworldly, unnatural, or creepy sense to it— it's a tree that makes children in to mentally retarded dwarves. That's… bad, yes, but kind of meh at the same time. Then I read the interview and I felt the author could have something here— if they add a bit more to the rest of the article aside from the interview.

Perhaps giving a reason why the mother feels that way— questions you might ask yourself could be… are her feelings towards her children supposed to be an oddity, or is she the norm and other mothers react the same way, and if so, why does it effect the mothers this way? Do the children tend to have specific behavioral traits beyond being mentally deficient?

A little bit more could go a long way.

I'm not saying you have to answer these questions, only that they're questions that the Foundation, as I understand it, would likely ask and be trying to find out, to see how much further they need to go to contain it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The interview is how you get readers to rethink what they just read.

upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept isn't bad, but its not that great either. It didn't really grap my attention too much, but other than that the interview of the story was top notch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After I figured out who the "real father" was, it struck me that this SCP-post is freaking genius! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Last sentence was kind of a lolwut.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps "control subjects" might be better than "normal subjects"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find the description to be fairly plain, but the interview is very well done.
I think some described examples of 1816-1 could be worth exploring, the inclusion of logs of some of the mentioned experiments could clear that up.
Summing up it's well written, clear + concise with the description and has a good ending interview but it is somewhat vague - more detail about 1816-1 would be a welcome addition.
I know that vagueness can be a good addition for some objects, but I think this is the kind that needs a bit more detail to really pop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find the description to be fairly plain, but the interview is very well done.
I think some described examples of 1816-1 could be worth exploring, the inclusion of logs of some of the mentioned experiments could clear that up.
Summing up it's well written, clear + concise with the description and has a good ending interview but it is somewhat vague - more detail about 1816-1 would be a welcome addition.
I know that vagueness can be a good addition for some objects, but I think this is the kind that needs a bit more detail to really pop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes. The third tree in that tale is the same tree. I was imagining different scenarios about where that tree could have come from and it led to that story. At first I thought of making it a tale for this wiki, but someone suggested the Library was better.

So the tree comes from a mysterious brotherhood of esoteric horticulturists. I'm still vague about whether the effect was created on purpose, but I prefer to think it was not intentional. This one was such a masterpiece that it was imbued by some of the ''soul'' of it's creator.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found this interesting enough for an upvote. though… is this possibly connected to this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the comments. I agree about the face and I decided to remove any mention of it. I will let people either see it for themselves or not.

It's true also about the room size. It was not mentioned clearly, but the idea is that the effect is blocked by walls. I changed the text to say that it's an underground room (just to be safe) and that the subject has to be in the same room. My original idea was to have it in an isolated little greenhouse, but I thought it would be safer to confine it in a windowless room.

I'm giving this SCP a break while I work on other stuff but I may improve it more later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see the face, but it isn't what I would consider humanoid. That's a matter of personal opinion to be sure, but I think it demonstrates the more important point; which is that it is not an appropriately clinical description.
The only way that it would fit the tone is if the similarity was striking and immediately apparant to all.

Containment seems iffy. The radius of effect extends well beyond the specified room dimensions, at least for mice. Since radius for humans is not fully understood, it would only make sense to err on the side of caution and assume a 10 meter minimum safe distance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, maybe if you could get a better picture or something…
Upvoted hesitantly, there is stuff there but it's a bit hard to catch. The fact that I don't know anything about penjings or bonsais doesn't help.
Also, what exactly is "murine"? Is that a fancy word for mice?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you; I see the problem with that sentence now.
I mean that they have a normal lifespan compared to normal individual. No subjects dies because of these defects, but they are not invulnerable.
The meaning of this is to emphasize that whatever transforms the subject is making sure that they are still viable.

I changed that sentence to: but instances of SCP-1816-1 invariably have a lifespan comparable to normal subjects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it.

"invariably survive into adulthood" - terrible phrasing. My first question was 'do you mean that they are unkillable and/or effectively immortal until adulthood'?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: because you didn't realize that "bonsai" is only the Japanese term, and the Chinese ones are called "penjing" ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't find it all that interesting. Not bad enough for a downvote, but not good enough for an upvote. I also agree that there's no human head in that picture. The "it makes bonsai people" thing is a good idea, but I don't think this is the best way to work it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It really doesn't catch my interest at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's a close-up of the 'face': http://scpsandbox2.wdfiles.com/local--files/dr-cuddles/face.JPG
The big hole on the left is the screaming mouth. The smaller hole on top of that is the nose. The slit in the middle-top is the eye and the squigly slit below that looks like the contour of the cheekbone. You sort of have to squint and twist your head a little, but it's there.

I sort of made up the name Zorkowicz, but: http://www.szarkowiczgroup.com/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reality is Unrealistic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That tree looks nothing like a humanoid head.
The name Zorkowicz is too ridiculous to be taken seriously.
Vote: down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you everyone. I will try to improve it, because it looks like it has a chance of surviving. I decided to play it Safe for my 1st one, so it's a bit subdued. For my next SCP, I may try something a little more complex.

(I probably won't have time tomorrow, but I will work on it this week-end)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't mean to add water to an already overflowing cup, but besides the interview log (which was the only unsettling part of the file for me) the rest was kind of meh; nothing about the description of a small tree drew me in.

Also, I think a few lines of your containment protocols could be reworded. As the procedure stands, it reads as a description of its current confinement, not as a 'how to' for containment. IE, "SCP-1816 is confined in a windowless experimental room measuring 7 x 7 x 4 meters" could instead read "SCP-1816 is to be confined in a windowless experimental room measuring 7 x 7 x 4 meters."

I like the idea, but pending a more interesting execution, no vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
OH GOD

WHY DIDN'T I REALIZE THAT UNTIL NOW

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok, concept is not to bad. But the execution was what killed it for me. It was boring and laid back. It was not put in a very interesting light. However, the interview was more interesting, and saved you from a downvote. If you can make it less laid back, and a little more interesting; you could earn an upvote from me. So no vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: oh my god it's doing bansai to people

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's actually pretty cool, so I'ma go ahead and upvote. I do think there could be a bit more about the tree itself though - have it show a bit more sentience, perhaps? It kind of feels like the babies are the only anomalous thing about the tree. Maybe some testing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not very interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you. I made the correction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
self-terminated

I remember you that the euphemism "terminated" is used in-universe to refer whenever a person/thing/cosmic horror/terrible SCP is killed or anihilated on porpuse by the Foundation. Otherwise, I'd recommend you to use "committed suicide".

I was hesitant till i read your explanation. I rather like your idea. +1 (I'll keep this upvote for now)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So this is basically asking how we'd feel if trees made bonsai people.

I like it. I feel like it could be a bit more interesting, but it's solid now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In a way, it's more similar to SCP-051. Here's how I see my idea and what makes it unique in my opinion:

If this severe effect of SCP-1816 was just caused randomly, it would be lethal in most cases. What I am trying to convey without saying it explicitely is that the tree may have some form of sentience and is doing it's stuff on purpose. Mrs Z. certainly believes that, but of course the reader is left to imagine whether she is right or not.

My original idea was that the bonsai was doing that out of spite and for revenge. Now I think more that it is doing it for artistic reasons. It's still up to the reader to decide.

It's a story about the relativity of beauty and horror and how one can be at the center of the other. Like Yin and Yang.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for your good words, it means a lot to me! Like Arachnoid says, the wording could be improved. I realized it takes a lot of talent and experience to write something both clinical and gripping.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not bad, but it's very similar to the Play Tubes SCP in that both cause pregnancy anomalies and congenital deformities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry I never got around to reviewing this for you. It's quite good, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Realization of what this was made it fairly unsettling. The wording isn't quite grabbing me, though. Unfortunately I'm not sure what exactly about the wording is problematic, there's just a lack of that spark.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for reading it. I'm sure I read 'self-termination' in other SCP's although I don't remember where right now. Of course I can easily change it to 'suicide' if that would be in better tone. It may not even need to be mentioned at all.

I tried to make the description as short as possible and still detailed enough. I can try to trim it some more. I want this one to be mostly a creepy story, not too scientific, so I could remove some things.

I think I could remove the mouse data or eventually put them in separate experimental reports.

(So far I am satisfied. My 1st attempt at an SCP was about at -15 by that time)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh. The main body of the thing was fairly boring, and the interview is what save this from a downvote. No vote.

EDIT: Also, the phrase "self-termination" is unbelievably awkward, and termination around here (I think) is used in very specific circumstances.
